name: Molina and Embid Castles Loop
name_original: Embid y La Yunta desde Molina de Aragón — circular por del Señorío de Molina
description: This 39.3-mile touring cycling route from Molina de Aragón is difficult, featuring 2147 feet of gain and historical castles.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Molina De Aragón offers routes through a landscape characterized by high plateaus, river valleys, and historical sites. The region features a mix of agricultural land, scattered forests, and open terrain, providing varied cycling experiences. Riders can expect undulating paths with moderate elevation changes, typical of the Iberian System foothills. The area's geology includes limestone formations and ancient riverbeds, shaping the routes available for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Molina De Aragón The most popular touring cycling route is The Three Taifas Trail, a 32.5 miles (52.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 32 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores historical connections across the landscape. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Molina and Embid Castles Loop, a difficult 39.3 miles (63.2 km) path. This route connects two significant historical castles, offering views of the surrounding countryside. Local touring cyclists also love the The Three Taifas Route: Molina de Aragón Loop, a 20.9 miles (33.7 km) trail leading through varied terrain including open fields and historical points, often completed in about 2 hours 16 minutes. Touring cycling around Molina De Aragón is defined by high plateaus, river valleys, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging long-distance routes. The routes in Molina De Aragón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from 9 reviews. More than 83 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Molina De Aragón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Molina De Aragón?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes available around Molina De Aragón, offering a variety of experiences through high plateaus, river valleys, and historical sites. These routes cater to different skill levels, though most are considered moderate to difficult.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners in Molina De Aragón?
A: While many routes in the region are geared towards experienced cyclists, there are options suitable for those new to touring cycling. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1188329827/the-three-taifas-route-molina-de-aragon-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Three Taifas Route: Molina de Aragón Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 33.7 km (20.9 miles) trail that can be completed in about 2 hours 16 minutes, offering varied terrain including open fields and historical points.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Molina De Aragón offers several challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1188267386/the-three-taifas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Three Taifas Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 52.2 km (32.5 miles) route with significant elevation changes, exploring historical connections across the landscape.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Molina De Aragón?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Molina De Aragón are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1188281520/molina-and-embid-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molina and Embid Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 63.2 km (39.3 miles) path that connects two significant historical castles.

Q: What kind of historical landmarks or attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history and natural beauty. Along the routes, you can encounter historical castles and religious sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31500541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Torete – Castle of Molina de Aragón loop from Molina de Aragón&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the impressive Castle of Molina de Aragón. You can also explore nearby attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2551153" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Hoz&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6928437" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pila de Villacabras&lt;/a&gt;, a historical site.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints along the touring cycling routes in Molina De Aragón?
A: Yes, the landscape of Molina De Aragón, characterized by high plateaus and river valleys, offers several scenic viewpoints. You can find stunning vistas such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5268357" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Hoces del Río Piedra from Mirador del Reconquillo&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4322842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Barranco de la Hoz&lt;/a&gt;, providing memorable stops during your ride.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in Molina De Aragón?
A: The best seasons for touring cycling in Molina De Aragón are typically spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these months, the weather is generally milder, with pleasant temperatures and less extreme heat than in summer, making for more comfortable rides. Always check the local forecast before heading out.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Molina De Aragón?
A: The touring cycling routes in Molina De Aragón are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's varied terrain, the historical landmarks encountered along the way, and the sense of exploration offered by the routes through agricultural land and scattered forests.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by natural features like gorges or caves?
A: Yes, the region around Molina De Aragón features impressive natural monuments and gorges. Some routes may bring you close to or offer detours to sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6385115" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cave of the Way of the Cross&lt;/a&gt; or the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7657727" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Puerta Cliffs&lt;/a&gt;, providing opportunities to explore the area's unique geology.

Q: What should I bring for a touring cycling trip in Molina De Aragón?
A: Given the undulating paths and moderate elevation changes, it's advisable to bring appropriate cycling gear, including a helmet, comfortable clothing, and sufficient water. Depending on the season, layers are recommended. Always carry a repair kit, a map or GPS device, and snacks. For longer routes, consider packing extra provisions as services may be sparse in some rural areas.

Q: Are there any less crowded touring cycling trails near Molina De Aragón?
A: While specific crowd levels can vary, many of the routes in Molina De Aragón traverse rural and less populated areas, offering a sense of tranquility. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31497603" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Torete – San Pedro Bridge loop from Corduente (núcleo)&lt;/a&gt;, a 56.5 km (35.1 miles) difficult route, often provide a more secluded experience away from main thoroughfares.
tips: Molina de Aragón, also known as the gateway between Castile and Aragon, is considered one of the most important points on the Camino del Cid and is a good base to explore the "Parque Natural del Alto Tajo". Even in his day, El Cid appreciated the hospitality shown to him by the Muslim ruler of Molina. He and his family enjoyed spending the night here on their journeys between Valencia and Castile. An overnight stay in Molina is just as worthwhile today as it was then, that's for sure.
Magnificent 12th century fortress, built by the Lord of Molina. Sitting on top of a defensive hill, the castle and its tower command views of the town and surrounding countryside.
La Yunta is a small and cute town on a plateau, surrounded by cereal fields and agricultural landscapes. Strange for a small town, it has a 14th century watchtower, Hermitage and a church.

The Embid castle is another fortified example of the power of the Molina lordship, protecting the trade routes to the north of the province of Guadalajara, bordering Teruel. A magnificent structure worth seeing.

faqs.question: Where does the Embid y La Yunta cycling route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in Molina de Aragón. As it's a circular route from a town, you should be able to find parking within the town itself to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a varied terrain, primarily designed for touring cyclists. The majority of the 63.2-kilometer trail, about 57.1 kilometers, is on paved asphalt surfaces. However, be prepared for approximately 5.72 kilometers of unpaved sections, along with smaller segments of different paved surfaces. This mix means a touring bike capable of handling both smooth roads and some rougher patches is ideal.

faqs.question: Is this cycling trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is not suitable for beginners. Komoot rates it as a "hard bike ride" that requires "very good fitness." The combination of its length (63.2 km), substantial elevation gain (650 m), and varied terrain makes it challenging.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Embid y La Yunta cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through the picturesque Señorío de Molina region, offering diverse natural landscapes and significant historical sites. You'll pass through Molina de Aragón, and can explore the Molina de Aragón Castle. Further along, you'll encounter La Yunta Village and Watchtower, and the historic Castle of Embid. The broader area, including parts of the Alto Tajo Natural Park, is known for its impressive river gorges, forests, and unique geological formations.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While the trail can be enjoyed in various seasons, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for cycling in the Señorío de Molina region. These seasons typically provide milder temperatures and beautiful scenery, avoiding the intense heat of summer or the colder, potentially icy conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Is the Embid y La Yunta cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in many outdoor areas, specific rules for cycling routes can vary. Given the length and difficulty of this particular touring bicycle route, it might be challenging for most dogs to keep up for the entire 63.2 km. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are fit enough for the distance and that you adhere to local leash laws, especially when passing through villages or near wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route, especially since it's near Alto Tajo Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or access the general area of the Señorío de Molina or the Alto Tajo Natural Park for recreational cycling. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected natural areas, and stay on marked paths. If you plan to visit specific attractions or facilities, check their individual requirements.

faqs.question: Does this cycling route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route does intersect with sections of other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Camino del Cid (Senderista), GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, and Las Tres Taifas for small portions of the journey, among other local streets and paths like Calle de los Adarves and Paseo de la Alameda.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the mix of paved asphalt (57.1 km) and unpaved sections (5.72 km), a touring bicycle is highly recommended. This type of bike is well-suited to handle both smooth roads and the occasional rougher patches you'll encounter, providing comfort and durability for the long distance and varied terrain.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Molina de Aragón, a town where you can expect to find various amenities including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. As you cycle through smaller villages like La Yunta and Embid, you might find more limited services, so it's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer stretches between towns.
